sound_type: Maximum insect emergence -- major Pacific Northwest river covered at peak spring
era: Peak spring (late April through May, water temperature 8-12 degrees C, photoperiod trigger). Annual event; scale depends on river health and intact cobble substrate.
ecosystem: Major Pacific Northwest river (Skagit, Columbia tributaries, Deschutes, Yakima, Snoqualmie type systems with intact cobble substrate)
season: Peak spring (Pteronarcys californica emergence late April-May; Skwala species earlier; Hesperoperla slightly later)
family: Order Plecoptera: Pteronarcys californica (Giant Salmonfly), Calineuria californica, Hesperoperla pacifica (Golden Stonefly), Skwala species

theory_nodes.concept: Plecoptera ancient order: 250-300 million year fossil record, Permian origin. One of most ancient winged insect orders. ~3,500 described species globally. Pacific Northwest river systems among most diverse stonefly communities in North America (100+ species in some systems). Stonefly presence = water quality indicator (most sensitive of aquatic invertebrates to degradation). Full-river emergence requires intact cobble substrate, adequate water quality, appropriate temperature regimes, minimal contamination. E=10 partly derives from rarity of systems capable of producing the event at this scale.
theory_nodes.description: Plecoptera -- ancient order, water quality indicator, emergence scale requires ecological integrity.

theory_nodes.concept: Life cycle and emergence mechanics: eggs in fast water over cobble; nymphal phase 1-4 years (Pteronarcys 2-4 years), 10-20+ molts, feeding in river; emergence trigger = water temperature reaching species-specific threshold (8-12 degrees C for Pteronarcys) + photoperiod (April-May); nymphs crawl to bank/rocks; exoskeleton shuck splits dorsally; adult emerges, expands wings, hardens 10-30 minutes. Mass emergence: thousands of nymphs per square meter of cobble simultaneously. River surface, banks, boulders, bridge pilings, streamside vegetation covered. At peak, adults cover water surface itself.
theory_nodes.description: Life cycle mechanics -- 1-4 years invisible, triggered emergence, river surface covered.

theory_nodes.concept: Acoustic profile: (1) Wing hum: Pteronarcys large wing area produces low buzz in flight; aggregate thousands = sustained hum filling riparian zone. (2) Oviposition strikes: females dipping to water surface repeatedly, thousands simultaneously = continuous acoustic texture across river width. (3) Fish feeding rises: trout, steelhead, whitefish feeding aggressively at surface, large fish producing substantial splashes. (4) River baseline: white noise of moving water amplified by acoustic reflectivity at close range. Combined = enveloping multi-source acoustic environment transforming river corridor from background to foreground soundscape.
theory_nodes.description: Acoustic profile -- wing hum + oviposition + fish rises + river baseline = enveloping environment.

theory_nodes.concept: Food web significance: mass emergence delivers enormous biomass to predator community -- Deschutes River Pteronarcys emergence estimated at millions of individuals over 4-6 weeks, each adult 0.5-2 grams, hundreds to thousands kg/river km total. Salmonid timing partially coordinated with invertebrate emergence periods. Protein input supports spawning energetics. The stonefly emergence is the most significant single-period prey delivery event in the river's annual cycle. E=10 from simultaneous acoustic maximum AND ecological maximum: these are not independent.
theory_nodes.description: Food web significance -- most significant single-period prey delivery event in annual river cycle.

theory_nodes.concept: Pairing rationale: stonefly nymph spends 1-4 years in river cobble, invisible, building toward emergence. Solger in Seattle 1980: underground, invisible except to people in the room, building the foundation of PNW hardcore before there was a scene to acknowledge it. Mass emergence = hidden foundation made visible all at once. Engine placing this pairing at degree 355 = the foundational acknowledgment: the thing everything rests on is ancient and patient and surfaces completely when conditions are right.
theory_nodes.description: Pairing rationale -- hidden foundation made visible, ancient and patient emergence.

cross_references.relationship: E=10 + E=10 = E=20. Stonefly nymphal invisibility (1-4 years in cobble) parallels Solger's underground existence before there was a scene. Mass emergence = hidden foundation made visible. The origination energy surfaces.
Previous entry. Simultaneous exterior detonation contrasts with patient foundational emergence. Closing sequence arc: exterior maximum → foundational emergence → intimate maximum.
Next entry. Shift from maximum number (millions of stoneflies, major river) to maximum density (ten wrens, one old-growth stand). Scale contracts; intimacy increases.

sps_pair.pairing_note: E=10 + E=10 = E=20. THE FIRST AND THE FOUNDATION. Both: ancient, patient, making themselves known completely when conditions are right. Stonefly nymph invisible 1-4 years then river surface covered. Solger in the underground before there was a scene, then the Seattle hardcore tradition covers everything that follows.

key_rivers: Deschutes River (Oregon); Skagit River (Washington); Yakima River; Upper Columbia tributaries; Snoqualmie River
